Article.ArticleTitle: Preparation and characterization of calcium ion-crosslinked carrageenan fibers with potential for wound dressing applications.

Abstract.AbstractText: Alginate-based dressings dominate the market, but carrageenan fibers-biocompatible, biodegradable, and bearing antibacterial sulfate esters-remain underexplored due to poor spinnability and poor mechanical properties of pristine carrageenan. To address these limitations, carrageenan spinning solution was pre-crosslinked with Ca2+, and Ca2+ content-property-structure relationships were systematically studied, with preliminary exploration of their use as medical dressings. Results of dynamic light scattering and viscosity analyses showed that addition of an appropriate amount of Ca2+ improved the stability of the spinning solution, optimized viscosity, and effectively reduced the average particle size of the spinning solution. Infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectrometry tests confirmed the formation of ionic bonds between the sulfate ester groups of carrageenan fibers and Ca2+. More specifically, in alkaline environment, an appropriate amount of Ca2+ led to in-situ formation of Ca(OH)2 precipitation, which effectively filled the internal pores of carrageenan fibers. Experiments showed that at a Ca2+ concentration of 4.6 mM, the obtained fibers (Ca2+-4.6 mM-CAF) exhibited optimal mechanical properties, including a breaking strength of 233.78 MPa and a breaking force of 19.32 cN. Furthermore, the introduction of Ca2+ also increased the crystallinity of the fibers to a certain extent. Polarized light microscopy revealed stronger birefringence, indicating improved molecular alignment of the fibers. Dressings from these fibers (Ca2+-4.6 mM-CAD) exhibited excellent performance: outstanding water absorption (swelling rate 2241.95 %), good air permeability (WVTR 55.21 %, porosity 56.76 %), and strong antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. The Ca2+ pre-crosslinking process was highly eco-friendly, offering new insights for green textile material development.
